answer:
**The *matla* (opening *sher* or couplet) of *Diwan-e-Zauq*, chapter 1 ("Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai"), is:**

> Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai  
> Jaan kashti qaza se ladti hai[^1]

**English translation:**

> My eye against her cruel and faithless way contends,  
> My soul against the ship of final fate contends.[^2]

This *ghazal* personifies various struggles ("ladti hai")—the eye with the beloved's cruelty (*pur-jafa*), the soul with destiny (*qaza*), and so on—culminating in the *maqta* referencing Zauq and the Arabic proverb "Al-harbu khid'atun" ("war is deceit").[^3]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch.1 ("Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai"), [transliteration](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/transliteration/).
[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch.1 ("Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai"), [p-c3221c](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/translation/#p-c3221c).
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch.1 ("Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai"), [p-c251f5](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/01-aankh-us-pur-jafa-se-ladti-hai/translation/#p-c251f5).
